hub_subtitle: Conversação para Feiras Internacionais de Turismo & Hotelaria

lesson.menu_title: Advising Clients and Handling Tricky Requests
lesson.menu_desc: Advise clients and handle difficult requests using the second conditional -- 28 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 10 -- Advising Clients
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Andrea Aggio | Aula 10 -- Advising Clients

listenings.text: Hi Andrea, it is Stefan again. I have a tricky one for you. My most important client wants a luxury trip to Italy for eight people, but he only has the budget for four-star hotels. He also wants a private guide in every city. Honestly, if I promised all of that at this price, I would lose money. But I do not want to say no. If you were in my shoes, what would you do? Maybe there is a smart alternative. Could you call me back with a recommendation? I trust your judgement. Thanks a lot, and talk soon.

Welcome to this short segment on the art of advice. When a client makes a request you cannot fully meet, never just say no. Instead, offer a smart alternative, and soften your advice with the second conditional. In English, we say, if I were you, I would choose the inland hotel, or if you had a little more time, you could visit both cities. Notice the pattern: if plus the past simple, and would or could plus the verb. This structure sounds polite and professional, because it presents your idea as a friendly suggestion, not an order. It also helps the client see the trade-offs clearly. So the next time you give advice, remember: if I were you, I would use the second conditional.

vocab.title: Match each word to its meaning
defs: another option you can choose instead
to suggest something is a good choice
a better room or service than the one booked
able to change or adapt easily
a balance between two things you cannot both have fully
to consider the good and bad points carefully
a calm feeling from knowing nothing will go wrong
specially designed for one client's needs
good enough to be worth the time or money
to think about a decision again

paras: In tourism, clients do not always want what is best for them. One asks for a beach in the rainy season; another wants five-star luxury on a three-star budget. A weak salesperson simply says yes and creates a problem later, or says no and loses the sale. A skilled advisor does something different: they offer a smart alternative and let the client decide.

The secret is in how the advice is given. Nobody likes to be told what to do. So experienced professionals soften their advice with a hypothetical: 'If I were you, I would travel in June instead.' 'If you chose the tailor-made package, you would have more flexibility.' This is the second conditional, and it turns an order into a friendly suggestion. The client keeps control of the decision, which makes them far more likely to say yes.

Good advice also makes the trade-offs visible. Every choice has an upside and a downside, and the client deserves to weigh them up honestly. 'The inland hotel is cheaper, but it is twenty minutes from the beach.' When you present the options this way, the client trusts you more, not less. In the end, the best advisors are not the ones who always say yes -- they are the ones who help the client make a decision they will not regret.
reading.source: Adapted from a hospitality business guide, The Trusted Advisor

reading.prompt: What is the main idea of the text?
choices: In tourism, you should always give clients exactly what they ask for.
Skilled advisors offer smart alternatives, soften advice with the second conditional, and make trade-offs clear.
The best salespeople never let the client make the final decision.

items: A skilled advisor offers a smart alternative instead of just saying yes or no.
The first paragraph says a skilled advisor offers a smart alternative and lets the client decide.
The text says the second conditional turns advice into an order.
It says the second conditional turns an order into a friendly suggestion.
Good advice makes the trade-offs of each choice visible to the client.
The third paragraph says good advice makes the trade-offs visible and lets the client weigh them up honestly.
'If I were you, I would travel in June' is an example of the second conditional.
The second paragraph gives this exact sentence as an example of the second conditional.

and give advice with the second conditional: 'If I
you, I would...'. They make the

gapfill.text: Second conditional = 'if' + past simple, then would/could + verb. Note: with I/he/she, use 'were' (If I were you), not 'was'. It talks about an unreal or hypothetical situation.

bank.label: Useful language for advising clients
items: If I were you, I would choose the low season.
If you booked earlier, you would get a better rate.
I would recommend the tailor-made package.
There is a trade-off: cheaper, but further away.
It would give your group peace of mind.
If you had more time, you could visit both cities.
Honestly, I would reconsider that option.
That option is more flexible and worthwhile.

lf.title: Complete the second conditional
items: If you had more time, you
If you chose the inland hotel, you
it, I would upgrade the rooms.

lf.text: The 'if' part uses the past simple (were, had, chose, allowed); the result uses 'would' or 'could' + verb. Use 'were' for all persons: If I were you...

items: A client wants a beach in the rainy season. Advise an alternative using 'If I were you, I would...'.
The client's budget is too low for their dream trip. Suggest a trade-off with the second conditional.
A client cannot decide between two hotels. Give a recommendation and make the trade-offs clear.
A client has a tricky, almost impossible request. Advise them honestly without simply saying no.

answerkey.title: Reveal the answer key
list: Gap-fill: 1 = alternative, 2 = were, 3 = trade-offs, 4 = weigh, 5 = trust.
Advice: 'If I were you, I would book the low season.' (were + would).
Option: 'If you had more time, you could visit both cities.' (had + could).
Never say 'If I was you, I will'. Say 'If I were you, I would'.
answerkey.note: Second conditional: if + past simple (were for all persons), then would/could + verb. Never 'will' or 'can' in the result.
